meta.notes: Physics (CPBS) electrostatics items. Stems and four options scraped verbatim from OpenStax College Physics for AP Courses 2e Ch. 18 'Test Prep for AP Courses' single-best-answer multiple-choice items. OpenStax publishes the answer key to instructors only, so each correctAnswer was independently verified from the same CC BY-NC-SA source text or by calculation (answerProvenance = verified-from-source-text). 'Select two answers' items, figure-dependent items (dipole/square/field-line diagrams), and free-response/numeric items were skipped. A few items in a figure-based series were included only where the answer is figure-independent (e.g., a polarized neutral conductor has zero net charge; the field between parallel plates is uniform).

questions.stem: When a glass rod is rubbed against silk, which of the following statements is true?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Electrons are removed from the silk.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Electrons are removed from the rod.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Protons are removed from the silk.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Protons are removed from the rod.

questions.explanation: Charging by friction transfers electrons, not protons (protons are bound in nuclei). Glass gives up electrons to silk, so electrons are removed from the rod, leaving the glass rod positively charged and the silk negatively charged.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 18 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (friction transfers electrons; glass becomes positive).

hints.prompt: Charging by friction (rubbing) transfers which particles between objects?

choices.label: A
choices.text: electrons (protons stay bound in the nuclei)

hints.rationale: Only electrons are transferred by friction; protons remain bound in the nucleus.

hints.prompt: Two of the answer choices say PROTONS are removed. Why can these be ruled out immediately?

choices.label: A
choices.text: protons are bound in the nucleus and are not transferred by rubbing

choices.label: B
choices.text: protons are lighter than electrons and leave first

hints.rationale: Friction transfers electrons, not protons, which are locked in the nucleus.

hints.prompt: Friction transfers electrons, never protons. Which pair of options can be eliminated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'Protons are removed from the silk.' and 'Protons are removed from the rod.'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'Electrons are removed from the silk.' and 'Electrons are removed from the rod.'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'Protons are removed from the silk.' and 'Electrons are removed from the rod.'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'Protons are removed from the rod.' and 'Electrons are removed from the silk.'

hints.rationale: Protons are not transferred by rubbing, so both 'protons removed' options are eliminated.

questions.stem: If objects X and Y attract each other, which of the following will be false?

choices.label: A
choices.text: X has positive charge and Y has negative charge.

choices.label: B
choices.text: X has negative charge and Y has positive charge.

choices.label: C
choices.text: X and Y both have positive charge.

choices.label: D
choices.text: X is neutral and Y has a charge.

questions.explanation: Attraction can occur between opposite charges or between a charged object and a neutral one (by polarization). Two objects with the same sign of charge always repel, so 'both positive' cannot produce attraction—that statement is false.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 18 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (like charges repel).

hints.prompt: Two objects can be attracted to each other if they are:

choices.label: A
choices.text: oppositely charged, OR one charged and the other neutral (by polarization)

choices.label: B
choices.text: only if both are positively charged

choices.label: C
choices.text: only if both are negatively charged

hints.rationale: Attraction occurs for opposite charges or for a charged–neutral pair (polarization).

hints.prompt: Two objects that carry the SAME sign of charge always:

choices.label: C
choices.text: exert no force on each other

hints.rationale: Like charges repel, so same-sign objects cannot attract.

hints.prompt: The question asks which situation CANNOT cause attraction (the false statement). Opposite-charge pairs clearly DO attract, so they are true statements. Which pair of options can be eliminated (because they describe real attraction)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'X has positive charge and Y has negative charge.' and 'X has negative charge and Y has positive charge.'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'X and Y both have positive charge.' and 'X is neutral and Y has a charge.'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'X has positive charge and Y has negative charge.' and 'X and Y both have positive charge.'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'X is neutral and Y has a charge.' and 'X has negative charge and Y has positive charge.'

hints.rationale: Both opposite-charge pairs genuinely attract, so they are true statements and cannot be the false one.

questions.stem: Suppose a positively charged object A is brought in contact with an uncharged object B in a closed system. What type of charge will be left on object B?
questions.explanation: Charging by contact (conduction) shares charge between the objects, so both end up with the same sign. Touching a positive object to a neutral one leaves the neutral object positively charged.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 18 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (conduction yields the same sign).

hints.prompt: Charging by CONTACT (conduction) leaves the two objects with:
hints.rationale: Conduction shares charge, so both end with the same sign.

hints.prompt: In CONTACT charging (not induction with grounding), does the touched object end up the same or opposite sign as the charged object?
hints.rationale: Direct contact shares charge, giving the same sign (opposite sign would require induction with grounding).

hints.prompt: Contact charging definitely transfers charge (so not neutral) and the outcome is predictable (so not 'cannot be determined'). Which pair of options can be eliminated?
hints.rationale: Contact transfers charge and the result is predictable, so 'neutral' and 'cannot be determined' are eliminated.

questions.stem: What will be the net charge on an object that attracts neutral pieces of paper but repels a negatively charged balloon?
questions.explanation: Repulsion only occurs between like charges, so repelling a negatively charged balloon means the object is also negative. (Attracting neutral paper happens for any charged object via polarization, so it doesn't distinguish the sign.)
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 18 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (repulsion requires like charge).

hints.prompt: Electrostatic REPULSION occurs only between:

choices.label: A
choices.text: two objects with the same sign of charge

choices.label: C
choices.text: a charged and a neutral object

hints.rationale: Only like charges repel.

hints.prompt: The object also ATTRACTS neutral paper. Does that attraction tell you the SIGN of the object's charge?

choices.label: A
choices.text: no—any charged object attracts neutral paper by polarization, regardless of sign

choices.label: D
choices.text: it means the object is neutral

hints.rationale: Attraction to neutral paper happens for either sign, so it doesn't reveal the sign; the repulsion does.

hints.prompt: Because the object repels the balloon, it must be charged (not neutral) and its sign IS determinable. Which pair of options can be eliminated?
hints.rationale: Repulsion shows the object is charged and its sign is determinable, so 'neutral' and 'cannot be determined' are eliminated.

questions.stem: In an experiment, a student runs a comb through his hair several times and brings it close to small pieces of paper. Which of the following will he observe?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Pieces of paper repel the comb.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Pieces of paper are attracted to the comb.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Some pieces of paper are attracted and some repel the comb.

choices.label: D
choices.text: There is no attraction or repulsion between the pieces of paper and the comb.

questions.explanation: Running the comb through hair charges it. The charged comb polarizes the neutral paper (inducing opposite charge on the near side), so the paper is attracted to the comb.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 18 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (charged comb attracts neutral paper by polarization).

hints.prompt: A charged object brought near small NEUTRAL objects will:

choices.label: A
choices.text: attract them (by inducing opposite charge on their near side)

choices.label: D
choices.text: give them the same charge and repel them

hints.rationale: A charged object polarizes neutral objects and attracts them.

hints.prompt: The paper bits start out neutral (uncharged). Could some of them be REPELLED by the charged comb?

choices.label: A
choices.text: no—neutral bits are only attracted (by polarization), never repelled

choices.label: B
choices.text: yes—neutral objects are repelled by charge

choices.label: C
choices.text: yes—about half attract and half repel

choices.label: D
choices.text: they are neither attracted nor repelled

hints.rationale: Neutral objects are attracted by polarization; repulsion would require them to be charged the same sign.

hints.prompt: A charged comb definitely affects neutral paper (so 'no effect' is out), and neutral bits aren't repelled (so 'repel' is out). Which pair of options can be eliminated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'Pieces of paper repel the comb.' and 'There is no attraction or repulsion between the paper and the comb.'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'Pieces of paper are attracted to the comb.' and 'Some pieces are attracted and some repel the comb.'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'Pieces of paper repel the comb.' and 'Pieces of paper are attracted to the comb.'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'There is no attraction or repulsion...' and 'Some pieces are attracted and some repel the comb.'

hints.rationale: The charged comb attracts neutral paper (no repulsion, and definitely an effect), so 'repel' and 'no effect' are eliminated.

questions.stem: Some students experimenting with an uncharged metal sphere want to give the sphere a net charge of the same sign as a charged aluminum pie plate. Which of the following steps would accomplish this?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Bringing the pie plate close to, but not touching, the metal sphere, then moving the pie plate away.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Bringing the pie plate close to, but not touching, the metal sphere, then momentarily touching a grounding wire to the metal sphere.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Bringing the pie plate close to, but not touching, the metal sphere, then momentarily touching a grounding wire to the pie plate.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Touching the pie plate to the metal sphere.

questions.explanation: Charging by conduction (direct contact) shares charge so the sphere ends up with the same sign as the pie plate. Induction with grounding (option B) would give the sphere the opposite sign, and simply approaching and withdrawing (option A) leaves it neutral.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 18 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (conduction gives same sign; induction+ground gives opposite).

hints.prompt: To give a neutral object the SAME sign of charge as a charged object, you use:

choices.label: C
choices.text: simply waving the charged object nearby and away

hints.rationale: Contact (conduction) yields the same sign; induction with grounding gives the opposite sign.

hints.prompt: Charging by induction WITH grounding produces which sign on the sphere, relative to the charged plate?
hints.rationale: Induction with grounding leaves the opposite sign, so it can't be used to get the same sign.

hints.prompt: Approaching then withdrawing leaves the sphere neutral, and induction with grounding the SPHERE gives the OPPOSITE sign—neither gives the same sign. Which pair of options can be eliminated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'Bringing the pie plate close..., then moving the pie plate away.' and 'Bringing the pie plate close..., then momentarily touching a grounding wire to the metal sphere.'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'Bringing the pie plate close..., then touching a grounding wire to the pie plate.' and 'Touching the pie plate to the metal sphere.'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'Bringing the pie plate close..., then moving the pie plate away.' and 'Touching the pie plate to the metal sphere.'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'Bringing the pie plate close..., then touching a grounding wire to the metal sphere.' and 'Touching the pie plate to the metal sphere.'

hints.rationale: Approaching then withdrawing leaves it neutral, and grounding the sphere during induction gives the opposite sign, so neither gives the same sign.

questions.stem: When a charged balloon is brought closer to a neutral metal sphere, there is a redistribution of charges within the sphere. What is this phenomenon called?
questions.explanation: The separation of charge within a conductor caused by a nearby charged object—without any charge being transferred—is called polarization (charge induction).
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 18 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim (figure reference removed; concept is figure-independent). OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified.

hints.prompt: When charges within a conductor rearrange due to a nearby charged object (with NO charge transferred), the phenomenon is called:
hints.rationale: Rearrangement without charge transfer is polarization/induction.

hints.prompt: Since the balloon never touches the sphere, can this redistribution be called CONDUCTION?

choices.label: A
choices.text: no—conduction requires direct contact to transfer charge

choices.label: D
choices.text: no—because no charges moved at all

hints.rationale: Conduction needs contact; here there is none, so it is not conduction.

hints.prompt: There IS a named effect here (so 'none of the above' is out), and with no contact it can't be conduction. Which pair of options can be eliminated?
hints.rationale: No contact rules out conduction, and there is a real named effect, so 'conduction' and 'none of the above' are eliminated.

questions.stem: A charged balloon is brought close to one side of an isolated neutral metal sphere, causing the charges in the sphere to redistribute. What is the net charge on the sphere?

choices.label: D
choices.text: It can be positive or negative depending on the material.

questions.explanation: Polarization only separates the existing charges within the isolated sphere; no charge is added or removed. The near side becomes one sign and the far side the opposite sign, but the net charge of the sphere remains zero.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 18 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. This item from a figure-based series is answerable without the figure (an isolated, polarized conductor has zero net charge). OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified.

hints.prompt: When a neutral, ISOLATED conductor is polarized by a nearby charge, its TOTAL (net) charge:

choices.label: A
choices.text: stays zero (charge is only separated, not added or removed)

hints.rationale: Polarization separates existing charge; the net charge stays zero.

hints.prompt: The near side of the sphere becomes one sign and the far side the opposite sign. Adding these together gives:

choices.label: A
choices.text: zero net charge (equal and opposite amounts)

hints.rationale: The induced positive and negative charges are equal and opposite, summing to zero.

hints.prompt: No charge is added to or removed from the ISOLATED sphere, so it cannot acquire a net positive or net negative charge. Which pair of options can be eliminated?

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'zero' and 'It can be positive or negative depending on the material.'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'negative' and 'It can be positive or negative depending on the material.'

hints.rationale: An isolated polarized conductor gains no net charge, so 'positive' and 'negative' are eliminated.

questions.stem: A positively charged rod is used to charge a sphere by induction. Which of the following is true?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The sphere must be a conductor.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The sphere must be an insulator.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The sphere can be a conductor or insulator but must be connected to ground.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The sphere can be a conductor or insulator but must be already charged.

questions.explanation: Charging by induction requires charges that are free to move and be redistributed (and typically a momentary ground connection), which only happens in a conductor. An insulator can only be polarized locally, not charged by induction.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 18 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (induction needs free charges, i.e., a conductor).

hints.prompt: Charging by induction requires that the charges in the object be:

choices.label: A
choices.text: free to move and be redistributed

hints.rationale: Induction needs mobile charges that can redistribute.

hints.prompt: Can an INSULATOR be charged by induction the way a conductor can?

choices.label: A
choices.text: no—an insulator's charges aren't free to move, so it can only polarize locally

choices.label: C
choices.text: yes, but only if already charged

hints.rationale: Insulators lack free charges, so they can't be charged by induction like conductors.

hints.prompt: Induction needs FREE charges (a conductor) and works on a NEUTRAL object (it need not be pre-charged). Which pair of options can be eliminated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'The sphere must be an insulator.' and 'The sphere can be a conductor or insulator but must be already charged.'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'The sphere must be a conductor.' and 'The sphere can be a conductor or insulator but must be connected to ground.'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'The sphere must be an insulator.' and 'The sphere must be a conductor.'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'The sphere can be a conductor or insulator but must be already charged.' and 'The sphere can be a conductor or insulator but must be connected to ground.'

hints.rationale: Induction requires free charges (not an insulator) and works on neutral objects (need not be pre-charged), so those two are eliminated.

questions.stem: Consider the (uniform) electric field created by two large, parallel, oppositely charged metal plates. Where is the electric field strongest?

choices.label: C
choices.text: At the midpoint between the plates

choices.label: D
choices.text: It is the same at all points between the plates.

questions.explanation: The field between two large parallel charged plates is uniform: it has the same magnitude and direction at every point in the region between them (ignoring edge effects).
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 18 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Based on OpenStax Figure 18.59; the labeled points are unnecessary because the field between parallel plates is uniform. Choices generalized to describe locations. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified.

hints.prompt: The electric field between two large, parallel, oppositely charged plates is:

choices.label: A
choices.text: uniform (same magnitude and direction everywhere between them)

hints.rationale: The field between large parallel plates is uniform.

hints.prompt: A student expects the field to be strongest right next to a plate (like near a point charge). Does that apply BETWEEN large parallel plates?

choices.label: A
choices.text: no—the parallel-plate field does not peak near a plate; it is uniform

choices.label: B
choices.text: yes—it peaks at the positive plate

choices.label: C
choices.text: yes—it peaks at the negative plate

hints.rationale: Unlike a point charge, the parallel-plate field is uniform, not peaked near a plate.

hints.prompt: In a UNIFORM field there is no special location where it is strongest, so options naming one plate as strongest are wrong. Which pair can be eliminated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'Near the positive plate' and 'Near the negative plate'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'At the midpoint between the plates' and 'It is the same at all points between the plates.'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'Near the positive plate' and 'At the midpoint between the plates'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'Near the negative plate' and 'It is the same at all points between the plates.'

hints.rationale: A uniform field has no strongest point, so 'near the positive plate' and 'near the negative plate' are eliminated.

questions.stem: The electrostatic force between two point charges is F. What will the force be if the distance between them is halved (charges unchanged)?
questions.explanation: Coulomb's law gives F ∝ 1/r². Halving the separation multiplies the force by 1/(½)² = 4, so the new force is 4F.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 18 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (F ∝ 1/r²).

hints.prompt: Coulomb's law says the electrostatic force depends on the separation r as:
hints.rationale: Coulomb's law is an inverse-square law, F ∝ 1/r².

hints.prompt: Since force ∝ 1/r², moving the charges CLOSER together (halving r) makes the force:
hints.rationale: A smaller separation gives a larger inverse-square force.

hints.prompt: Bringing the charges closer INCREASES the force, so any answer smaller than F is wrong. Which pair of options can be eliminated?
hints.rationale: Closer charges feel a larger force, so F/4 and F/2 (smaller than F) are eliminated.

questions.stem: For two point charges with electrostatic force F between them, which of the following statements is false?

choices.label: A
choices.text: If the charge of one of the particles is doubled and that of the second is unchanged, the force will become 2F.

choices.label: B
choices.text: If the charge of one of the particles is doubled and that of the second is halved, the force will remain F.

choices.label: C
choices.text: If the charge of both particles is doubled, the force will become 4F.

choices.label: D
choices.text: None of the above (all three statements are true).

questions.explanation: Coulomb's law gives F ∝ q₁q₂. Doubling one charge gives 2F (A true); doubling one and halving the other gives 2 × ½ = 1, so F is unchanged (B true); doubling both gives 2 × 2 = 4F (C true). All three are correct, so the false statement is 'none of the above.'
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 18 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (all three scaling statements satisfy F ∝ q₁q₂).

hints.prompt: Coulomb's law says the force between two point charges is proportional to:

choices.label: A
choices.text: the product of the two charges, q₁q₂

choices.label: B
choices.text: the sum of the two charges

hints.rationale: F ∝ q₁q₂ at a fixed separation.

hints.prompt: Using F ∝ q₁q₂: doubling one charge multiplies F by 2; doubling one and halving the other multiplies it by (2)(½) = 1; doubling both multiplies it by (2)(2) = 4. How many of these three statements are correct?
hints.rationale: Each scaling follows F ∝ q₁q₂, so all three statements are correct.

hints.prompt: The question asks which statement is FALSE. Since F ∝ q₁q₂, doubling one charge really does give 2F and doubling both really does give 4F—so those statements are TRUE. Which pair of true statements can be eliminated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'doubling one charge (other unchanged) gives 2F' and 'doubling both charges gives 4F'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'doubling one and halving the other leaves F' and 'None of the above (all three statements are true).'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'doubling one charge gives 2F' and 'None of the above (all three statements are true).'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'doubling both charges gives 4F' and 'doubling one and halving the other leaves F'

hints.rationale: Both of those scaling statements are correct (true), so neither can be the false statement being sought.

questions.stem: Two massive, positively charged particles are initially held a fixed distance apart. When they are moved farther apart, the magnitude of their mutual gravitational force changes by a factor of n. By what factor does the magnitude of their mutual electrostatic force change?
questions.explanation: Both Newton's law of gravitation and Coulomb's law follow an inverse-square dependence on distance (∝ 1/r²). So the same change in separation scales both forces by the same factor n.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 18 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (both forces are inverse-square in r).

hints.prompt: Both Newton's law of gravitation and Coulomb's law depend on the separation r as:
hints.rationale: Both gravity and the Coulomb force are inverse-square in r.

hints.prompt: Since BOTH forces have the SAME 1/r² dependence, the same change in separation scales them by:
hints.rationale: Identical distance dependence means identical scaling factors.

hints.prompt: Because both forces share the identical 1/r² distance law, the electrostatic force changes by the SAME factor as gravity—not that factor squared. Which pair of options can be eliminated?
hints.rationale: Identical distance dependence gives the same factor, not a squared one, so 1/n² and n² are eliminated.

questions.stem: Two particles with charges +2q and +q are separated by a distance r. The +2q particle produces an electric field E at the +q particle's location and exerts a force F on it. What is the electric field of the +q particle at the location of the +2q particle, and what force does it exert on the +2q particle?
questions.explanation: Field is proportional to the source charge, so the +q particle (half the charge of +2q) produces half the field, E/2, at the same distance. By Newton's third law, the force the +q particle exerts on the +2q particle is equal in magnitude to F.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 18 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (field ∝ charge; forces equal by Newton's third law).

hints.prompt: The electric field a charge produces at a given distance is proportional to:

choices.label: A
choices.text: the size of that source charge

choices.label: B
choices.text: the size of the test charge feeling it

hints.rationale: A source's field is proportional to its own charge (at fixed distance).

hints.prompt: The +q particle has HALF the charge of the +2q particle. At the same distance, the field it produces is:
hints.rationale: Field ∝ source charge, so half the charge gives half the field.

hints.prompt: You found the +q particle produces field E/2 at the +2q particle. Which pair of options can be eliminated because they list the field as E (full strength)?
hints.rationale: The +q particle's field is E/2, not E, so both options listing the field as E are eliminated.

questions.stem: Two particles with charges +2q and +q are separated by a distance r; the +2q particle exerts a force F on the +q particle. When the +q particle is replaced by a +3q particle (at the same location), what will be the electric field and the force from the +2q particle experienced by the +3q particle?
questions.explanation: The electric field created by the +2q particle depends only on that source and the distance, so it is unchanged at E. The force on the new particle is F = qE, and tripling the charge (to +3q) triples the force to 3F.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 18 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (field set by source; force ∝ test charge).

hints.prompt: The electric field created by a fixed source charge at a given point depends on:

choices.label: A
choices.text: only the source charge and the distance (not on the test charge placed there)

choices.label: C
choices.text: both the source and the test charge

hints.rationale: A source's field is independent of the test charge; it depends only on the source and distance.

hints.prompt: The +2q particle's field is unchanged when the other particle is swapped. Since force = charge × field, tripling the test charge (+q → +3q) makes the force:
hints.rationale: Force = charge × field, so tripling the charge triples the force.

hints.prompt: The +2q particle's field at that location is set by the +2q source, so replacing the other particle leaves the field at E (unchanged). Which pair of options can be eliminated because they reduce the field to E/3?
hints.rationale: The field from +2q is unchanged (E), not E/3, so both 'E/3' options are eliminated.

questions.stem: The direction of the electric field of a negative charge is:

choices.label: A
choices.text: inward (toward the charge) at all surrounding points

choices.label: B
choices.text: outward (away from the charge) at all surrounding points

choices.label: C
choices.text: inward on one side and outward on the other

questions.explanation: By convention, electric field lines point toward negative charges. The field of a negative point charge therefore points radially inward (toward the charge) at every surrounding point, independent of any test charge.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 18 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Options reworded from the source's test-charge phrasing to clearly describe field direction (single best answer preserved). OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (field points toward a negative charge).

hints.prompt: By convention, electric field lines point:

choices.label: A
choices.text: away from positive charges and toward negative charges

hints.rationale: Field lines point away from positive charges and toward negative charges.

hints.prompt: A student says the field of a negative charge points OUTWARD (like a positive charge's). Why is that wrong?

choices.label: A
choices.text: field lines point TOWARD negative charges, so the field points inward, not outward

choices.label: B
choices.text: it's correct—all charges have outward fields

hints.rationale: Only positive charges have outward fields; negative charges have inward fields.

hints.prompt: A single point charge has a nonzero, purely radial field (not zero, and not different on two sides). Which pair of options can be eliminated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'inward on one side and outward on the other' and 'zero everywhere'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'inward (toward the charge) at all surrounding points' and 'outward (away from the charge) at all surrounding points'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'inward on one side and outward on the other' and 'inward at all surrounding points'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'zero everywhere' and 'outward at all surrounding points'

hints.rationale: A point charge's field is radial and nonzero, so 'mixed directions' and 'zero everywhere' are eliminated.

questions.stem: The force responsible for holding an atom together is:
questions.explanation: The attraction between the negatively charged electrons and the positively charged nucleus is electric (electrostatic). This electromagnetic force binds the atom; gravity is far too weak at atomic scales.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 18 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (electron-nucleus attraction is electric).

hints.prompt: The force that binds electrons to the nucleus in an atom is:

choices.label: A
choices.text: the electric (electrostatic) attraction between opposite charges

hints.rationale: The electron–nucleus attraction between opposite charges is electric.

hints.prompt: Why is gravity NOT the force holding the atom together?

choices.label: A
choices.text: gravity is far too weak at atomic scales compared with the electric force

choices.label: B
choices.text: gravity is repulsive between the electron and nucleus

choices.label: C
choices.text: gravity does not act on electrons

choices.label: D
choices.text: gravity is actually the strongest force in atoms

hints.rationale: Gravity is negligibly weak between subatomic particles compared with the electric force.

hints.prompt: Friction is a macroscopic contact force (not relevant to atoms) and gravity is far too weak at atomic scales. Which pair of options can be eliminated?
hints.rationale: Friction is macroscopic and gravity is negligibly weak in atoms, so both are eliminated.

questions.stem: When a positively charged particle exerts an inward (attractive) force on another particle P, what will be the charge of P?
questions.explanation: An inward (attractive) force toward a positive charge means P is attracted to the positive particle. Since unlike charges attract, P must be negative.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 18 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (attraction to a positive charge ⇒ P is negative).

hints.prompt: Two charged particles ATTRACT each other when their charges are:
hints.rationale: Opposite charges attract; like charges repel.

hints.prompt: If P were also POSITIVE, what would happen between it and the positive particle?

choices.label: D
choices.text: they would attract only at large distances

hints.rationale: Two positive charges repel, so P being positive contradicts the observed attraction.

hints.prompt: A same-sign (positive) P would repel, and the outcome IS determinable from the attraction. Which pair of options can be eliminated?
hints.rationale: A positive P would repel (not attract) and the sign is determinable, so 'positive' and 'cannot be determined' are eliminated.
